replies.text: "Slingshots" like the one David used were known to antiquity as far away as Rome and even the British isles, and one can get really good and accurate with it. Studies exist comparing David's sling to a modern sidearm. So yes... David brought a gun to a swordfight.

author: @HowardFyvie
text: I live in Cape Town and know the team who created this movie. It took Phil 10 years to pull it off - as a standalone studio based in Noordhoek, Cape Town, they persevered and kept on believing. I went to a premiere of it last month and was blown away by how good it was. Angel Studios jumped on board to help, they got writers on board from The Chosen, and they roped in some of the best of the best here in South Arica. Super proud of them and amped to see how it goes! Has the potential to be the new 'Prince of Egypt' . Go watch it!

replies.text: @s@sherrihamptonmusicwith respect, while a real battle probably took place involving a large Philistine warrior and an Israelite hero (likely Elhanan or David), the specific narrative of David and Goliath in the Bible is a story crafted to convey a powerful message about faith and divine intervention. The story of David and Goliath is generally considered a mythification of actual events rather than a strictly literal, historically accurate account as written. While King David was likely a real historical figure, many scholars view the specific narrative of his battle with Goliath as a legend with a basis in reality, embellished over time for theological and nationalistic purposes. Highly unlikely to have happened as written in the biblical account if indeed at all. ❤

replies.author: @skyp613
replies.text:
​@SweatyBapsI just want to note that in philosophy, atheism is indeed defined as the proposition that God does not exist, which is a claim that carries a burden of proof. If you hold that there isn't sufficient evidence to justify God's existence or non-existence, that's agnosticism. 

I realize that the word atheism is commonly used the way you used it but I do find the above definitions offer more clarity.

Also, it is commonly said that you can't prove a negative but that isn't actually true. For instance, the law of non-contradiction. If you prove that A is True, you have also proven that A is not False. And for a more grounded example, if I claim I have a 5 ton elephant in my bathroom, you can reasonably prove I don't by walking in my bathroom and looking.

replies.author: @skyp613
replies.text:
​kawasukiyamahonda"There is no such thing as magical Jews."
That's a strawman.

"The Torah, Bible, and Quran are part of Middle Eastern mythology and are therefore fictional literature."
That's not actually an argument. All you did was say that these books are false (mythology) therefore they are false (fiction).

"Let me guess, you try to prove that Harry Potter is real by referencing other Harry Potter books."
Nope, that would be circular like the argument above. If I were to make the case for Christianity, I would start with philosophical arguments for the existence of God (cosmological argument, fine tuning of the universe, moral argument) then give historical evidence for the resurrection of Jesus (crucifixion, empty tomb, testimony of the eyewitnesses in the face of persecution).

These evidences are being grappled with by the experts of their respective fields so I would just be cautious and try to avoid the overconfidence associated with the start of the Dunning–Kruger curve which seems to be very common with internet atheists.

replies.author: @salvadorromero9712
replies.text:
I want to see a Samson that depicts him as an ordinary looking guy, perhaps even an undersized one like David as he slays his enemies. His strength didn't come from genetic gifts or training his muscles; it was a supernatural gift from God promised as long as he kept to his Nazirite vows. As soon as his hair is cut it vanishes. Nowhere in the Bible does it mention him as a man of any height, bulk, or otherwise remarkable appearance.

It really undermines the character, both in its religious message, and simply as a compelling character, for Samson to be this big brawny behemoth. (As well as make it downright confusing, particularly after he gets his haircut but still has his muscles.) And yet, even in religious instructional materials, he's always depicted that way. It's like someone just one day (presumably in the early modern period) thoughtlessly and crudely decided to give him the standard visual representation of physical might; and everyone just rolled with that convention from then on, too unthinking or timid to challenge it. Weird stuff.

author: @GuadalupePicasso
text:
Being a kids movie, is it safe to assume that they didn’t include the scenario where David, now king, sees Beersheba bathing on the roof, and straight up decides to bed her. She gets pregnant, and David calls her husband, Uriah,  back from war, in the hopes that they’ll be intimate such where he’ll hopefully think that the baby is his. Alas, Beersheba’s husband is a man of honor, and won’t even sleep in the same bed as his wife (I believe that biblical narrative states that he slept in the doorway of their house), saying that, for so long as his brothers-in-arms were still at battle, that he could partake of no pleasure. So, what does David do? He sends Uriah to the front lines, knowing full well that it was a death sentence.

A prophet named Nathan straight up calls David out for his double sin, and David repents, and writes Psalm 51 which is in the Bible, as his prayer of repentance (look it up; one doesn’t have to be religious to know that it’s a work of art).

replies.author: @DedicatedSpartan
replies.text:
I would like to do the story of David, always have with a serious tone. I would do a series as David's story is far too rich to be covered in one movie. Phase one David is a youth that is the least appreciated son ( without going into to much detail, knowing the culture of Israel it was not an accident that Jesse did not bring him to meet Samuel when Samuel called for his sons) to the kings shield bearer and plays the haro to calm him. Then he is a warrior leader l, outcast again and in the run for years while Saul hunts him. He becomes a mercenary to the Philistines. Then he becomes King, partially, he fights a civil war against Abner for the thrown before he is even crowned in Jerusalem. David then peeks and becomes strong and powerful rights the wrongs by punishing those whom betrayed him in the past, rewards those who helped him when he was nothing ( a very important part of his character I will go deeply into). Then he sets down the path of lust, that leads him to murder & all that he has built is threatened and judged to be punished by God as he David watches. This is where the tragedy theme of the story has always been wasted in my opinion. It is a great story of self reflection and being humbled by your own wicked actions, that does not need to be preached at the audience. 

Of course I would not make the same foolish choice other writers do with David and that is cut away that he was a warrior king & remove all of the action. Kings command, they order, they judge, they govern, they punish they start and end wars. Most live action Davids nearly touch on this when it comes to David's character. 

I could go on but those are some of my thoughts on how I would tell David's story and what I do not like about how it has been told in film.

author: @Laughy-Flaaffy
text:
I want more biblical animated films that take on the maturity and sacredness captured in Dreamwork’s  _The Prince of Egypt._ I really believe that film is the strongest animated biblical film of all time and a downright cinematic masterpiece. This David film feels like a step in the right direction, but after seeing _Angel Studio’s_ trailer for the new animated _Animal Farm,_ I’m lacking confidence in that entire company now.

EDIT:
Just got done seeing it, and I loved it! The film is surprisingly great! I wish they had included a bit more detail at the end with Saul’s conclusion. My friend who was unfamiliar with the Bible said _”Wait, so what happened to Saul?”_ since the movie never shows any indication of his wrap up. I know you can’t show Saul shish kabobbing himself on the battlefield, but they could have implemented the _Witch of Endor_ scene from the book of Samuel to show a clear (and none violent) wrap up to Saul, Jonathan, and even Samuel’s story. The film basically just didn’t show any indication of what happened to those central characters, with the exception of Jonathan falling off his horse in battle.

author: @DoomKrakken
text:
I saw this movie in theaters a couple days ago, not even knowing what to expect.  Free tickets paid forward, invited by another family with kids, I hadn't heard of the movie (thought it was gonna be something more along the lines of _Kingdom of David,_ more live-action).

My gosh, it _blew me away._

*_We're so back, fellas._*

All the kiddos were super excited from start to finish, I kept looking over to see their reactions, _they were glued to the screen._  Longer runtimes are great when they're filled to the brim with quality content.  By the time it finished, I was surprised it had ended, and it felt like it ended too soon.  I truly wanted more.

Some corrections from Drinker's breakdown of the movie:
- David killed many lions and bears to defend his flock, _as was his job as a shepherd._  This was the norm, this was to be expected.
- David's great gift was for _music,_ not for slaying a lion with a slung rock... and he was summoned to King Saul's court to bring him comfort with his music, not to become an advisor.
- David used a _sling,_ not a _slingshot,_ to sling his rocks.  Slingshots rely on elastic bands and/or the flexibility of a handle to use tension to send a projectile flying... but _slings_ are even simpler devices swung to maximize kinetic energy, relying only on the user's dexterity, skill, and strength.

I think there's more, but that's all I have for now.

replies.author: @MalcomTyler
replies.text:
​@KrazzeeKaneCalling the Bible “just a book like any other” isn’t an argument—it’s a dodge.
Yes, humans physically wrote it. No serious Christian disputes that. The claim has never been “God held the pen,” but that God worked through human authors over centuries. If “written by humans” disqualifies truth, then history, law, science, and philosophy collapse with it.
Multiple translations don’t prove malleability—they prove preservation. The core theology, narrative, and moral framework remain intact across thousands of manuscripts. In fact, the Bible is one of the most textually consistent ancient works in existence. Variation doesn’t weaken it; it shows how seriously it was copied and transmitted.
Saying it’s “only as true as believers wish it to be” confuses belief with reality. Gravity doesn’t stop working if you don’t believe in it. Truth isn’t voted on.
And calling it “not special” ignores its actual impact: it shaped civilizations, legal systems, human rights concepts, art, ethics, and philosophy for millennia. No other book has done that at anything close to the same scale. You don’t have to accept its claims to acknowledge it’s categorically different.
You’re free not to believe it—but dismissing it as “just a book” isn’t skepticism. It’s intellectual laziness dressed up as confidence.

replies.author: @SepticFuddy
replies.text: @Quincy_Morris I've seen the first season and don't know where the "woke" accusation is coming from. It's "inaccurate" in a similar way as The Chosen, where they're adding in a lot of stuff to try to give more sense of flavor of the setting rather than stick strictly to the narrative details in the text. Personally, as someone who's a stickler for the textual details, I actually appreciate that they're departing enough to make it clear that they're not trying to give an accurate account but rather a compelling sense of immersion to make the overall story more relatable. Makes it easier to approach it as entertainment bringing the story to life as opposed to a faulty Bible study. The usual preaching approach of pretending to present the text accurately while having to invent and change a whole lot just to tell it and then presenting it from a modern and juvenile cultural lens is what annoys me about most Biblical adaptations. House of David does not whitewash the bloodstained nature of the Biblical accounts like everything else does, which I find refreshing. Joshua and Judges would be neat to see in such a format. It DOES modify key details, some intentionally and some perhaps unwittingly. I also appreciate that from what I've seen so far, it's not inserting any retroactive Christian narrative insinuations but rather tries to deal with the story on its own terms

Exodus 21:7–11 doesn’t endorse sex slavery—it prevents it. The girl is not a chattel slave but a protected bride-in-training. The law restricts the man, forbids resale, demands marriage-level provision, and guarantees her freedom if mistreated. In a world where women had virtually no rights, this law elevated them. replies.author: @ArchivistAdrift
replies.text: ​kawasukiyamahondaIn the agrarian ancient Near East, without modern social support structures like welfare programs, families in extreme poverty often faced brutal choices like starvation or debt servitude just to survive. Exodus 21:7-11 describes one such regulated arrangement: A father could place his daughter as a servant with the understanding that she might become the wife of the master or his son. This provided financial relief for the family and a secure future for the daughter.If no marriage occurred, she could not be sold to foreigners and remained redeemable by her family. If marriage did take place, she was no longer to be treated as a servant/slave, but given the same rights as any other full-fledged wife: food, clothing, and marital relations (verses 10-11). If these were denied, she went free without payment.Israel's laws on this were far more merciful than those of surrounding cultures, offering unique protections for the girl's dignity. The Bible doesn't command or celebrate the practice. Instead, it tightly regulates an existing harsh reality while reminding Israel of their own history as slaves. Over time, Scripture pushes toward greater freedom, seen in things like the Jubilee release every 50 years and New Testament teachings that slave and free are equal in Christ.

@michaelguinnee-sh9xd I actually think that sort of thinking takes away from the poignancy of the story.
The whole idea is that God can use whatever or whoever he wants to accomplish his goals. To use the meekest, smallest, seemingly least capable to do the extraordinary is what shows how truly great God is. 
Sure, David loved God with all his mind, but I don’t think that is what got David the win. David could have been a complete numbskull, and God still could have given him the victory. 
David was tiny, couldn’t wear his brother’s armour, and couldn’t swing a normal size sword, but God still gave him the strength and courage to go out and beat Goliath, because that is how powerful faith in our Lord truly is.

author: @bloopboop9320
text:
The story of David actually gets more interesting and complicated the further you go on. Usually, people stop at David and Goliath or they stop at Bathsheba, but you really have to go all the way to the end to see him suffer the consequences of his actions. By the end of David's life, one of his best friends betrays him due to the Bathsheba incident, joins forces with David's son, Absalom, who also hates David due to him not holding another one of David's sons accountable for raping their sister, and they start a civil war and kick David out of the kingdom. David tries to reconcile with his son on several occasions, but David's top general, Joab, puts an end to all of it by finally killing David's son so that ANOTHER civil war didn't start from all of David's soldiers being mad at how little David showed care for his own troops.

However, down the road, that same general, Joab, starts to kill other leaders that were appointed to positions of power that formerly fought Joab in the war. This eventually leads to David making sure that Joab is executed due to his cold pragmatic reasoning and lack of ability to try and forgive and repair bonds between the two groups in the civil war.

author: @SeraphsWitness
text:
This narrative actually wasn't condensed, it was EXPANDED. Between David fighting Goliath and his ascension to the throne, there's actually very little narrative to draw from in 1-2 Samuel where the story can be found. They added quite a bit of.... well... artistic flourish to the story, especially at the end. 

David & Goliath isn't about "brains versus brawn". It's about trusting in the Lord. He makes that pretty clear in the movie and in the bible.

They added quite a bit, so I encourage anyone to actually read the account. It's not that long, and 1-2 Samuel (along with the whole bible) are free online.

Major plot points they got right:
- David's anointing by Samuel
- David & Goliath
- Saul's descent into madness and his reliance on David to soothe him with his music
- Saul's eventual chasing of David into the wilderness
- The cutting of Saul's robe (and the tearing of Samuel's robe as a symbol for Saul's kingdom being torn from him.

They left out quite a bit as well. David is actually on the run from Saul for many years. And the whole thing with the Amalekites is almost entirely contrived at the end.  They also really de-emphasized David's friendship with Jonathan, who were actually best friends. They spent a great deal growing close, and Jonathan was actually the one who warned David to flee from his father. I wish they'd included that.

It was a decent adaptation. Albeit a very forced ending.

replies.author: @ikkinwithattitude
replies.text:
​@thequeenjessI actually thought that the movie's use of the Amalekites was very well-considered.

It does a number of things: 
- Presents the patristic interpretation of the Amalekites as an embodiment of spiritual darkness (Bishop Barron brings up this interpretation of the Saul-Amalek incident and Saul's failure to "hack Agag to pieces" every time he comments on dark passages in the Bible!) in a way that kids can understand by making them terrifying demon-people who enslave David's loved ones
- Directly connects Saul's failure to exterminate the Amalekites with the loss of his kingdom by having the Amalekites force David to abandon Saul 
- Allows David to win on his own terms by inspiring his flock to be courageous and have faith in God (recalling his failed attempt to do so with the literal sheep at the beginning)
- Shows David as a "man after God's own heart" by portraying him as a thoroughly Christ-like figure (the visuals scream "Crucifixion") who wins by faith rather than strength

One can argue that the choices made were more theme-driven than narrative driven, but I find it hard to blame them for it when it's so thematically perfect. 

Besides, there was no big victorious fight to end on in the source material, so ending on a spiritual victory won at the cost of a physical loss is probably the best option available (especially since the primacy of the spiritual over the material is referenced repeatedly throughout the movie). 

The other option -- securing a viable retreat from the Philistines through trickery but losing Jonathan and Saul anyway -- would have been way less satisfying, and much too dark for children.

author: @CarParts08382
text: Wifey and I took our kids to see the flick. While it was cleaned up for the target demographic compared to what the details of the Bible say (such as Goliath's head being severed following his defeat by David), it was a Cliff's Notes of the story of David's beginnings that lead into his young adult life. It told the story that summarizes it very well & my kids sat through it without any issues.

author: @AzuraSkyy
text:
Watched half of this video when you dropped it- went to see the film and am coming back to finish the video.
Haven't been to church in over a decade but David was a delight not as a religious story but just as an incredible film with pretty solid music. My wife especially loved the cute animals and music but it has some good heart felt movies and you are spot on with the writers getting out of their own way- Music was not forced when the drama needed time to settle nor were jokes inserted to ruin the darkest moments.

I plan to see it again with coworkers.

author: @DiBaozi
text:
I took my young kids and they enjoyed it! They didn’t think it was too long. 
My thoughts as a mom 
1) the music has to hit and music did! It was catchy and moving and inspiring.
2) quality Animation on par with The Incredibles- such detail in the tapestry scenes. 
3)No ambiguity- A clear scary villain (instead of a misunderstood villain) , a clear protagonist, and justified conflict. 

No cheesy sermon yet it stuck with most of the source material adding creative liberties that didn’t take away from the story of David’s life. Also definitely designed for G rating, no blood, or implied, no romance nor implied.
